+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 0
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
Judge Evaluation:
[Skipping – generation error: Text too short after 3 attempts]
+ Iteration 2 — Avg Score: 83.0
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 79.5
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four: The Antares Overture Arthur kept adjusting his collar, unused to the formal attire he was instructed to wear. The synthetic weave itched at his neck where the magnetic clasp pinched, and the maroon tunic — issued to him by a silent attendant moments after boarding — fit a fraction too snugly across the shoulders. He supposed that was the point. A man in borrowed clothes does not forget whose hospitality he enjoys. The transfer vessel hummed beneath him, a low sub-audible vibration that spoke of Kleinmann drives running hot. Through the narrow viewport, Mars had dwindled to a rust-colored coin, and the hard white speck of Phobos was sliding past with the indifferent grace of something that had been doing this for four billion years. "Twelve minutes to rendezvous, Mr. Neegan," said the steward, a compact man with the trim posture of an ex-serviceman and a sidearm he did not bother to conceal. The insignia on his breast — three interlocked rings pierced by an arrow — was the sigil of the Antares Diplomatic Corps. Arthur had seen the emblem twice in his life before today: once on a news-spool covering the Ceres Incident, and once on a gutted corvette being towed into Deimos yards with its crew compartment vented to space. Diplomats, he thought, who traveled armed and left no survivors to negotiate with. "Rendezvous with what, exactly?" Arthur asked, for perhaps the third time. The steward smiled the small, practiced smile of a man who had been trained to smile like that. "With your host, sir." --- The host's vessel was not shown on any commercial registry Arthur recognized. It was long and dark and elegant in the way a stiletto is elegant — narrow of purpose, broad of consequence. The docking was so smooth Arthur only knew it had occurred when the artificial gravity shifted by half a percent and a green indicator winked above the airlock. He was escorted through three corridors, two checkpoints, and one antechamber paneled in what appeared to be genuine Terran oak — an extravagance that would have bought his entire homestead twice over — before being shown into a drawing room. The room was an anachronism. A reading lamp of brass and green glass burned on a mahogany desk. A globe of Mars, spun on a gimbal, showed the old pre-settlement cartography in faded ink. The man behind the desk was rising to meet him: tall, silver-templed, with the unhurried motions of someone who had never in his adult life been required to hurry. "Mr. Neegan. Thank you for coming. Do sit. The chair is more comfortable than it appears, which is perhaps the opposite of most things in our line of work." He gestured to a leather wing-back. "My name, for the purposes of this conversation, is Mr. Vale. Brandy? It is real. I have it sent up from Kentucky, which is an absurd extravagance I justify by telling myself I am preserving a craft." "I'll pass," Arthur said, lowering himself into the chair. "I'd like to know why I'm here." "Directness. Good. I had been told you were an engineer, and engineers, in my experience, prefer the shortest line between two points." Vale settled back, steepling his fingers. "You are here, Mr. Neegan, because four days ago a core sample from Lot 447-Grid-Epsilon registered an Azra concentration of eleven point four percent by mass. Do you know how many verified deposits in the known system exceed ten percent?" "I could guess." "Guess." "Three." "Two. Yours is the third. The other two are, respectively, under the direct administration of the Combine and garrisoned by eight hundred marines of the Terran Directorate." Vale smiled thinly. "Yours, at present, is guarded by a chain-link fence and a dog named — I believe — Hector." "Rickover." "I stand corrected." Vale inclined his head with genuine amusement. "Rickover. A fine name for a dog sitting atop the most consequential mineral find of the decade." Arthur allowed a silence to grow. He had negotiated enough mineral rights in his career to know that the man who spoke first after a number was disclosed generally lost. Vale seemed to appreciate the gambit; his smile widened by a calibrated fraction. "Very good. Very good indeed. Let me tell you what I am authorized to offer, and then let me tell you what I am *not* authorized to offer, because the second list is in some ways more instructive than the first." "Please." "The Antares Consortium is prepared to purchase your lease outright for seventeen million credits, to relocate you and any designated family to a world of your choosing within a fifty-parsec radius, and to grant you a perpetual royalty of one-half of one percent on gross Azra yield. That figure, I should note, would make you — within perhaps eighteen months — wealthier than the Governor of Mars." "And what are you *not* authorized to offer?" Vale's eyes brightened. "I am not authorized to guarantee your safety should you decline. Not because we would harm you — we would not; it would be poor form and worse policy — but because we would no longer be in a position to prevent others from doing so. You understand, I hope, that you have become a piece on a board, Mr. Neegan. The question is not whether you will be played. The question is by whom." "Who else is playing?" "The Combine. The Directorate. A certain cartel operating out of the Jovian Trojans whose name I will not speak aboard my own vessel. And" — here Vale paused, and for the first time something like real seriousness entered his face — "something rather newer. We have reason to believe Azra does not merely accelerate decay. We have reason to believe, under certain configurations, it may *reverse* it. Do you grasp what that would mean?" Arthur's mouth had gone dry. "Stable transuranics." "Or stable *anything*. A universe in which entropy itself can be negotiated with." Vale lifted his brandy. "You see now why the fence and the dog will not suffice." Arthur looked down at his hands. They were, he noticed, perfectly still. "I'd like to hear the offer again," he said. "Slowly."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence and successfully captures many elements of Asimov's style. The author effectively establishes the retrofuturistic atmosphere through careful details like "Kleinmann drives," synthetic weave clothing, and the juxtaposition of advanced technology with classical furnishings. The world-building operates on both macro and micro levels as requested, from the personal details of Arthur's discomfort in borrowed clothes to the galactic implications of entropy reversal. The dialogue is particularly well-crafted, with Vale's sophisticated, measured speech patterns contrasting effectively with Arthur's more direct engineering mindset. The conversation feels natural while efficiently conveying exposition about the political situation and the true significance of Azra. Vale emerges as a nuanced antagonist - polite, cultured, and genuinely appreciative of Arthur's intelligence, yet clearly dangerous. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from Arthur's initial disorientation through the intimidating display of wealth and power to the revelation that fundamentally changes the stakes. The author skillfully plants hints about larger conspiracies and dangers without overwhelming the immediate scene. However, some elements feel slightly overwrought. Phrases like "the indifferent grace of something that had been doing this for four billion years" about Phobos, while poetic, edge toward purple prose. The description of the ship as "elegant in the way a stiletto is elegant" is effective but borders on cliché. The characterization is solid but not exceptional. Arthur's reactions feel believable for an engineer thrust into political intrigue, though his personality could be more distinctive. Vale is well-executed as the urbane manipulator archetype but doesn't transcend the type significantly. The piece adheres closely to instructions, delivering the requested scene structure, dialogue focus, and Asimovian elements. The revelation about entropy reversal provides genuine surprise and raises the stakes dramatically. The ending maintains appropriate tension without false positivity. Minor weaknesses include some telling rather than showing in character descriptions and a few moments where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious in its literary aspirations. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 85.9
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Darkroom Revelations The red light makes everything look like a wound. I watch Elliot's hands move through the developer bath, his fingers long and careful, nails painted a black that's chipped in ways that look intentional. He's wearing that oversized mesh shirt again, the one with the stretched-out neck that slips off his shoulder and reveals the sharp wing of his collarbone. I've catalogued that collarbone for fourteen weeks now. I've made it into a whole thing in my head. "The contrast is off," he says, voice low. He always talks quietly in here, like the chemicals might overhear. "Let me see." I lean over his shoulder, close enough to smell his shampoo—something herbal, unisex, the kind that comes in a brown bottle. I usually would've made a move by now. Usually, by week three of obsessing over someone, I've got them pressed against a wall somewhere, laughing at how surprised they look. That's my whole deal. Dani with the undercut, Dani with the leather jacket, Dani who knows what she wants. But Elliot makes me want to go slow in a way that's unfamiliar. Almost uncomfortable. Like learning to write with my left hand. "You could push it another thirty seconds," I say, and I don't touch him, even though my whole body is tilted toward his like a sunflower. He nods. Agitates the tray. The image rises through the paper—a self-portrait, his own face half-obscured by his hands, eyeliner smudged in a way that could be crying or could be styling, could be both. "Dani." He's looking at the photograph, not at me. "Can I tell you something weird?" My phone buzzes in my back pocket. Probably Mikayla, probably asking if I want to come out tonight. I ignore it. I've been ignoring a lot of things lately. "Always." He lifts the print with tongs, moves it to the stop bath. His shoulder blades shift under the mesh. "I don't know if I'm a boy. I mean—" He laughs, a small puff of air. "I don't know if I'm *only* a boy. Or if I'm a boy the way people mean it. I've been thinking about it since, like, high school, but lately it's been—" He gestures at the photograph. "Loud. In my head." The darkroom goes so quiet I can hear the timer ticking. My first instinct is to say *I knew it*, which would be wrong, and presumptuous, and also not quite true—I didn't *know*, I just noticed things, the way his eyes lingered on the femme kid in our crit group, the way he'd touched his own throat once when a professor said *young men like you*. My second instinct is to pull him into a hug, which would also be wrong, because he's balanced on the edge of something and I don't get to tip him either direction. My third instinct, the one that actually comes out of my mouth, surprises me. "Thank you for telling me." He finally looks up. Under the safelight his face is all shadow and blush-colored planes, and his eyes are wet, but not in a sad way. In a seen way. "I haven't said it out loud before," he says. "Like. To a person." "How does it feel?" "Terrifying." A pause. "Good." I lean my hip against the counter. I'm usually so sure of the choreography of these moments. I know when to step in, when to make the joke, when to kiss someone so they stop being nervous. But Elliot isn't nervous the way people usually are around me. He's nervous the way you are when you're handing someone something breakable that you made yourself. "Do you want me to use different words for you?" I ask. "Different name, different—" "He's still fine. For now. I think. I don't know." He laughs again, helpless. "I don't know what I want. Sorry. This is so—" "It's not." "I just—" He sets down the tongs. "I didn't want to say it to someone who'd make it a *thing*. And you're so—you know what you are. You're so you. I thought maybe you'd know how to hear it without." *Without making it a thing.* I feel something turn over in my chest. He sees me as solid. As a person who has her whole deal figured out. And here I am, quietly, privately falling apart over him in a way I haven't told anyone, not even Mikayla, because I don't have language for it yet. I like girls. I like butch girls and femme girls and the sharp-jawed boys I pick up at shows because they remind me of butch girls. I have a *type*, and Elliot isn't it, except he is, except what does that even mean, except maybe my type was always softer than I gave it credit for. "I don't always know what I am," I say, and it comes out before I can smooth it into something cooler. "I'm figuring it out too. Just—in my own corner. Quietly." He blinks. "Really?" "Don't look so shocked." "I'm not shocked. I'm—" He shakes his head, smiling. "Relieved." We stand there in the red light with the ticking timer and his half-fixed photograph and my phone going still in my pocket. I want to touch him. I want to ask if I can. I want to let him decide—not because I've gone shy, but because for the first time in a long time, wanting someone feels like *listening* instead of *reaching*. "Can I—" I start. "Yeah," he says, before I finish. I take his hand. Just that. His fingers, damp from the chemistry, cold, shaking a little. I lace mine through his and we both look down at them like they're a photograph coming up in the tray, slow and strange and unmistakably there. "Okay," he whispers. "Okay," I say. The timer goes off. Neither of us moves.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive yet introspective character navigating unfamiliar emotional territory. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically, creating an intimate confessional atmosphere that serves the story's themes of identity exploration and vulnerability. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's internal conflict between her usual take-charge approach and her desire to give Elliot agency feels authentic and well-developed. Her self-awareness about her own identity questions adds compelling complexity. Elliot emerges as a fully realized character despite limited dialogue, with his vulnerability and tentative self-discovery rendered believably. The prose quality is consistently high, with evocative imagery ("The red light makes everything look like a wound") and precise sensory details that ground the reader in the scene. The writing avoids melodrama while maintaining emotional weight. Metaphors like "learning to write with my left hand" effectively convey Dani's disorientation without feeling forced. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, capturing the hesitancy and careful navigation of sensitive topics. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from observation to revelation to tentative connection. The ending strikes an appropriate balance—hopeful without being unrealistically resolved, acknowledging that both characters are in process rather than providing false closure. The piece successfully explores themes of gender identity, sexual orientation questioning, and the complexity of attraction without being didactic. It shows rather than tells emotional states through physical details and internal monologue. The chemistry between characters develops organically through shared vulnerability rather than manufactured tension. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward being slightly too polished for the character's voice, and some metaphors that, while effective, occasionally call attention to themselves. The piece could have pushed slightly deeper into Dani's own identity questioning, though this may be a constraint of the word limit. + Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Stolen Moments in Roma ## I. Trevi, 10:47 AM The Vespa's horn shrieked like a wounded bird, and Ben Harcourt—Oxford don, author of three unread monographs on Renaissance hydraulics—leapt backward into a fountain railing, his guidebook pinwheeling into the air. "*Stronzo!* Are you blind or just English?" She straddled the bike like it was a throne, all black leather and tangled dark hair, a courier bag slung across her chest. Her helmet dangled from the handlebar. "I'm—I'm terribly sorry—*mi scusi, mi scusi molto*—" "*Molto?*" She laughed, head thrown back. "*Molto* is for wine. You say *tanto.* '*Mi scusi tanto.*' But better you say nothing, because your accent is a crime against my ears." Ben fumbled for his book, now splayed open in a puddle. "Yes. Right. I'll—shut up, then." She studied him a moment, head tilted. Something softened, or sharpened—he couldn't tell which. "Englishman. What's your name?" "Ben." "*Ben.*" She tried the syllable like a sweet she wasn't sure she liked. "Get on." "Sorry?" "The bike. *Andiamo.* You nearly kill me, so now you owe me a morning." "I nearly—*I* nearly killed *you*?" She was already handing him the spare helmet. ## II. The Market at Campo de' Fiori, 11:23 AM She wove the Vespa through alleys Ben was certain were pedestrian-only, ignoring his small yelps at each cobblestone. At the market she dismounted and pulled him by the wrist between stalls piled with artichokes and blood oranges. "Taste this." A wedge of pecorino appeared in her fingers. Ben took it obediently. "Did you pay for—" "Sssh." A peach appeared next, pressed to his mouth. He bit. Juice ran down his chin, and her thumb caught it with an efficiency that felt entirely unprofessional. "Alessandra," he said, the name still new on his tongue, "I really think we ought to—" "Ought to what?" She produced, from somewhere inside her jacket, a small silver spoon engraved with a hotel crest he did not remember seeing her near. She tucked it back before he could speak. "You think too much, Ben. It's all over your face. Like a rash." "That's not—" "Come. I know a church nobody goes to." ## III. Santa Maria dell'Orazione, 12:40 PM Inside, it was cool and smelled of cold stone and old wax. A single nun dozed in a pew. Alessandra crossed herself with a speed that suggested long familiarity and zero reverence. "You believe in God, Englishman?" "I believe in... syllabi." She laughed, too loud for the space, and the nun stirred. "What do you study?" "Water systems. Aqueducts, fountains. How Rome drinks." She looked at him then—really looked, as if he had become briefly three-dimensional. "So you came all this way to learn about the plumbing." "When you say it like that—" "I say it like it is." She slid closer on the pew. He could smell her shampoo, something citrus and sharp. "And what do you do for pleasure, Professor Plumbing?" "I... read." "*Dio mio.*" She pressed her palm to her forehead in mock despair. "We must fix this before sundown." ## IV. The Rooftop, 2:15 PM How they got onto the roof of the apartment building on Via dei Coronari, Ben would never quite reconstruct. There had been a door propped with a brick, a stairwell, her finger pressed to her lips. Now Rome spread below them in terracotta and gold, and Alessandra was lying on her back on the hot tiles, eyes closed, smoking a cigarette she had almost certainly not bought. "This isn't your building, is it." "No." "Alessandra." "Ben." She opened one eye. "In England, do people die of being slightly uncomfortable? Is it an epidemic?" "Yes," he said. "It's called the national character." She laughed, and it was the first time he heard her really laugh—not the performance laugh, the scoffing one, but something surprised out of her. He filed it away like a manuscript fragment. ## V. The Downpour, 4:52 PM The sky cracked without warning. One moment the air was thick and coppery; the next, the rain came down in sheets, hammering the cobbles, soaking her hair flat against her skull before they'd moved ten feet. She grabbed his hand and ran, and Ben—Ben who had not run in public since 1998—ran. They skidded under the awning of a gelateria on some narrow street he couldn't name. Water sluiced off the canvas in silver ropes. Alessandra was breathless, laughing, her white t-shirt rendered entirely transparent, and Ben was heroically attempting to look at the gutter. "You're blushing," she observed. "I'm British. It's a circulatory condition." She stepped closer. Close enough that he could see a freckle beside her mouth he hadn't noticed before, and the rain on her eyelashes, and the way she was looking at him—amused, yes, always amused, but underneath it something more appraising. More careful. "Ben." "Yes." "Why did you get on the bike?" He considered the question honestly, which was perhaps his great failing. "Because you told me to. And because I have been in Rome eleven days and nothing has happened to me. And then you happened." Something flickered across her face. For half a second, the mischief dropped, and what was left underneath was quieter and, he suspected, much more dangerous. Then she kissed him. Quick, tasting of peach and cigarette and rain. Her hand was in his jacket pocket when she pulled back, and she removed it holding his hotel key card, which she twirled once between her fingers. "Tonight," she said. "Nine o'clock. Piazza Navona, by the river god with the bad face. Wear something that is not that shirt." She darted out into the rain before he could answer, the key card tucked into her bra, her laugh trailing behind her like a scarf. Ben stood under the awning for a long time, smiling at nothing, a man comprehensively and happily robbed.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative flair in crafting a contemporary romance set in Rome. The author successfully creates two distinct, compelling characters whose personalities emerge clearly through dialogue and action rather than exposition. Alessandra's mischievous energy and Ben's scholarly awkwardness feel authentic and create natural chemistry. The structure works well, using numbered scenes with timestamps to create a cinematic quality that moves the narrative forward at a brisk pace. The dialogue is particularly strong - Alessandra's voice feels genuinely Italian in its rhythm and directness, while Ben's British politeness and self-deprecating humor ring true. Lines like "I believe in... syllabi" and "It's called the national character" showcase wit without feeling forced. The author handles the kleptomania element subtly and effectively - the stolen spoon, cigarette, and key card feel organic to Alessandra's character rather than gimmicky. The progression from near-collision to kiss feels earned through their interactions, particularly the moment where Ben honestly explains why he got on the bike. The prose strikes a good balance between literary quality and accessibility. Descriptions like "The Vespa's horn shrieked like a wounded bird" and "Rome spread below them in terracotta and gold" create vivid imagery without becoming overwrought. The rain scene is particularly well-executed, using the weather to create intimacy and vulnerability. However, there are some minor weaknesses. The piece occasionally relies on familiar romance tropes (the mysterious, dangerous woman; the repressed academic), though it executes them well. Some transitions between scenes could be smoother - the rooftop scene arrival feels slightly abrupt. The ending, while charming, borders on being too neat with the key card theft serving as both romantic gesture and character revelation. The emotional complexity is handled well - Alessandra's motivations remain appropriately enigmatic while Ben's attraction feels genuine rather than purely physical. The cultural clash is portrayed authentically without resorting to stereotypes. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 82.8
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three: The Cephalopod at Closing Time The gravity hiccups again and every glass on the back bar does that little shimmy, like they're all sighing at once. Third time tonight. I tap the stabilizer panel with my knuckle out of habit, even though we both know it won't do anything. The Outpost is older than my grandmother and twice as stubborn. "Pello's gonna have to look at that again," Mux says from the end of the bar, not looking up from his cards. "Pello looked at it last week." "Pello looks at everything every week. That's different from fixing it." I'm about to fire something back when the door hisses and a stranger walks in like she's being chased by her own shadow. Keldari, by the look of her — those tentacled dreads that usually coil neat against the scalp are doing something frantic, half-unbraided, one of them still twitching like it can't decide which direction danger is coming from. She scans the room in that quick, practiced way people do when they've learned the hard way that exits matter. Then she drops onto the stool farthest from the door but closest to the service corridor. I know that seat. That's the *I might need to leave through the kitchen* seat. "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Her voice is low, smoke-scratched. I pour. The amber wobbles in the glass as another gravity blip rolls through, and she watches it like it's trying to tell her something. "Rough orbit?" I ask. "You could say that." "That's what I said." She almost smiles. Almost. Takes the double in two swallows that would make a Drennari deckhand wince, then sets the glass down very precisely, like she's placing a bet. "Another." "That's your nerves talking. They're gonna hate you in the morning." "They already hate me. Another, please." I pour. I've learned, over my years behind this bar, that there's a specific species of trouble that walks in wearing someone's skin, and there's regular trouble, and then there's the trouble that's still *following*. This one is the third kind. I can smell it on her — not literal, just that ozone-prickle I get. "Nets," Mux says, warning in his voice. He's seen it too. "I know, Mux. Play your cards." "I'm playing against myself." "Then you're definitely losing. Focus up." The stranger huffs a laugh through her nose. "You always talk to your customers like that?" "Only the ones I like." "You don't know me." "No, but you tipped me with a smile, so you're off to a better start than half this room." She pulls the second whisky closer but doesn't drink. Her tentacles have settled a little, curling loose around her shoulders. One of them keeps flicking toward the door, though. Involuntary. Like a nervous tic that got a mind of its own. "You got a name?" I ask. "Depends who's asking." "The woman pouring your drinks, mostly." A pause. "Sable." "Sable. Nice. I'm Nettle. Nets, if you stick around." "I won't." "Everyone says that." I start polishing a glass because it gives my hands something to do while I read her. The dreads tell me she's been running at least two days without proper rest — they get duller when a Keldari's sleep-deprived, and hers look like pewter where they should look like wet obsidian. The cuff of her jacket has a tear that's been hastily sealed with med-foam instead of stitching. And she keeps touching her left side, light, checking, like there's something tucked there she can't believe is still there. "So," I say, casual, "what's in Vega whisky country bringing you out this far?" "Nothing's bringing me. I'm just going." "Going where?" "Away from." "Ah." I nod. "One of those." "One of those." The door hisses again and she stiffens so fast I feel it from across the bar. But it's only Pello, dragging in with that gait of his, already griping about the gravity plate before he's fully through. Sable exhales. Her shoulders drop maybe a centimeter. "Nets! I swear on the Twelve, that stabilizer is—" "In your hands tomorrow, Pello, I know. Drink?" "Rhuban tea. With the thing." "With the thing, coming up." I pour Pello's tea and flick in the bitter syrup he pretends nobody notices him ordering, and when I turn back Sable is staring into her whisky like there's a map at the bottom of it. "You want to tell me what you're running from," I say quietly, "or do you want me to guess?" "I want you to pour me a third and mind your bar." "Fair enough." I pour. The gravity burps and her glass wobbles, whisky climbing the sides like it's thinking about escape. She catches it with a fingertip, steady as surgery. Whatever she is, she's got hands that have done precise work under pressure. She notices me noticing. "You don't miss much," she says. "I miss plenty. I just don't miss the people who walk in looking like you do." "And how do I look?" "Like somebody's coming." Her eyes meet mine properly for the first time, and they're this startling molten copper, and for half a second I see the shape of whatever she's running from reflected in them — not details, just the *size* of it. Big. Bigger than a bar fight. Bigger than a debt. "If somebody comes in asking about a Keldari woman," she says, very evenly, "you didn't see one." "Sable—" "I'll pay for the lie. I've got chits." "It's not about chits." "Then what's it about?" I look at her, this stranger with her nervous dreads and her steady hands and her whole life apparently folded into whatever's pressed against her left ribs. The gravity skips again. Every glass in the room trembles together, a small chorus of wobbling. "It's about," I say slowly, "whether I want to know what I'm lying about before I agree to lie." She looks at the door. Then back at me. "You don't," she says. "But I think you're going to anyway."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering a cozy sci-fi bar scene in first person present tense with approximately 60% dialogue as requested. The author establishes clear conflict through Sable's mysterious arrival and hints at pursuing danger without resolving it within the chapter. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Nets emerges as a perceptive, experienced bartender with a protective streak, while Sable is convincingly portrayed as someone on the run - tense, guarded, but not without moments of dry humor. The supporting characters like Mux and Pello feel authentic to the setting. The dialogue flows naturally and reveals character efficiently, with each voice feeling distinct. The world-building is subtle but effective. Details like gravity hiccups, the aging Outpost infrastructure, and the casual mention of various alien species create a lived-in feeling without overwhelming exposition. The author shows restraint in revealing information, letting details emerge organically through character interactions and observations. The prose strikes an appropriate balance - descriptive enough to create atmosphere without becoming overwrought. Phrases like "walks in like she's being chased by her own shadow" and the description of Sable's tentacled dreads create vivid imagery while maintaining the cozy, conversational tone. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from routine bar banter to the gradual revelation that Sable represents serious trouble. The author maintains mystery while providing enough concrete details to keep readers grounded. Some minor weaknesses include occasional exposition that feels slightly forced, and a few descriptions that border on trying too hard for effect. The ending, while appropriately unresolved, could have pushed the tension slightly further. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 85.0
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Gone South The guy in the pickup dumps me on Canal Street like I'm a sack of something he forgot he was hauling. "Good luck, kid," he says, and the way his eyes slide off me makes my skin crawl retroactively, like maybe I dodged something worse than I knew. I grab my backpack. The door slams. He's gone. 6:47 AM. Phone at 34%. The air hits me like a wet towel across the face — I didn't know air could *be* this, thick and green-smelling and already warm even though the sun's barely up. Tennessee humid is nothing. This is something else. I walk because standing still feels worse. My binder's been on for nineteen hours and my ribs are starting to remind me that's against the rules. Everything aches. I haven't cried yet. That's the one thing I'm proud of. *Mom's face, when I told her. That's not grief, I realize now, replaying it. That's disgust trying to pass for grief.* Stop. Keep walking. I find a McDonald's and sit in the corner with a small coffee I can barely afford, watching my phone like it's a dying hamster. Three missed calls from Mom. One from Dad. Nothing from Jesse, which hurts in a specific way I don't have time for. I turn off location services. I turn off everything. 22%. "You gonna eat that?" I look up. There's a girl — maybe girl, maybe not, I'm trying to unlearn assumptions — standing by my table pointing at the hash brown I forgot I ordered. She's got a shaved head growing out uneven, a nose ring, and a hoodie that says CAMP WINNEBAGO 1997 even though she can't be older than seventeen. "Take it," I say. She sits down across from me without being invited and eats the hash brown in three bites. Her fingernails are painted black and chipped to hell. "You just got here," she says. Not a question. "How can you tell?" "You're doing the thing." She gestures at my whole self. "The deer thing. Eyes. You'll get robbed in like an hour if you keep doing it." She wipes her fingers on her jeans. "I'm Marguerite. Not my real name. Don't ask." "Taylor." "That your real name?" "...Yeah." "Huh." She studies me. Her eyes are that pale blue that looks almost white in certain light, and I can't tell if she's reading me or just tired. "Runaway?" I don't answer. "That's a yes. Okay." She leans back. "Rule one: don't sleep in Louis Armstrong Park. Rule two: the dude who hangs out by the Walgreens on Decatur will offer you a place to crash. Don't. Rule three —" "Why are you telling me this?" She shrugs. "Because someone told me. You eat your breakfast the whole way through or you don't make it to lunch down here, kid." "I'm sixteen." "I'm nineteen," she says, and she's lying, I can tell, but I let it go. --- Marguerite — I keep thinking of her in quotation marks — takes me on what she calls the tour. It's mostly a list of places *not* to go. We end up at the river, the Mississippi, which is bigger and browner than I ever pictured. I thought rivers were supposed to look like something. This one looks like it's thinking about eating us. "You running from something or toward something?" she asks, legs dangling over the edge. "From." "Yeah, me too, mostly." She pulls a squashed pack of cigarettes out of her hoodie pocket and lights one with a hand that shakes just a little. "Sometimes I tell people I'm going to L.A. but I'm not going to L.A. I'm just going." "I came out to my parents," I say, before I decide to say it. "Two days ago." She looks at me sideways. "Came out how." "Trans." She nods slow. Doesn't make a face. Doesn't perform anything. "Which direction?" "I don't — not a girl. Probably a boy. Maybe neither. I don't know yet." "Okay," she says. Like I told her what I ordered at McDonald's. "Taylor still works." Something in my chest does a weird small thing. I look at the river so she doesn't see my face. Phone at 16%. --- By noon the heat is a physical assault and my binder is a crime against my torso. Marguerite takes me to a place she calls "the drop-in," which is a church basement with fans and outlets and a bored-looking woman named Dez who doesn't ask questions but hands me a granola bar and points at a charger. I almost cry over the charger. I do not cry over the charger. While my phone nurses itself back to life, Marguerite disappears into the bathroom for a long time. When she comes out her eyes are red and she won't look at me. "You okay?" I ask. "Don't," she says. Not mean. Just — a door closing. She sits down and puts her head on the table. After a minute she says, to the table, "My mom's boyfriend. Okay? That's my thing. That's what I'm running from. I'm telling you because you told me yours and I don't want you to think you're the only one with a story. But we are not talking about it again." "Okay." "Okay." She keeps her head on the table. I watch her shoulders move. *Who do I trust?* I think. *Her? Dez? Nobody?* The answer, I'm starting to figure out, is: you trust in increments. You trust one hash brown at a time. You trust the person who doesn't ask which direction. My phone buzzes. 47%. A text from Jesse: *are you alive please just say you're alive.* I stare at it for a long time. The church basement hums. Marguerite breathes into the table. Somewhere outside a brass band is warming up, one sour note at a time, and it sounds — weirdly — like the beginning of something. *alive,* I type back. Send.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in depicting a transgender teen's first day as a runaway in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a blend of vulnerability, defensiveness, and observational acuity that feels genuine for a 16-year-old in crisis. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the piece follows Taylor through their first day, introduces another runaway (Marguerite) with clear secrets and issues, maintains first-person mixed tense throughout, and incorporates both internal monologue and dialogue effectively. The 1000-word target is met precisely. Character development is particularly strong. Taylor's voice remains consistent - sardonic yet vulnerable, observant yet overwhelmed. The internal monologue feels authentic, especially moments like the retroactive skin-crawling about the truck driver and the specific hurt about Jesse not calling. Marguerite is well-crafted as a character who serves as both guide and fellow damaged person, with her own protective mechanisms and boundaries. The dialogue is naturalistic and serves multiple functions - advancing plot, revealing character, and building the relationship between Taylor and Marguerite. The exchange about coming out is particularly well-handled, with Marguerite's matter-of-fact response ("Which direction?") feeling both realistic for someone in her position and emotionally significant for Taylor. The prose demonstrates strong imagery without becoming overwrought. Descriptions like the humid air hitting "like a wet towel across the face" and the Mississippi looking "like it's thinking about eating us" are vivid and character-appropriate. The writing maintains an appropriate level of literary sophistication without feeling forced. The emotional complexity is well-executed. Taylor's conflicting feelings about their situation, family, and new circumstances are layered throughout. The piece avoids both excessive sentimentality and unrealistic toughness, finding a believable middle ground. The pacing works well, moving through the day in logical segments while building the relationship with Marguerite. The ending strikes an appropriate note - not overly optimistic but suggesting possibility, which feels earned given the day's events. Minor weaknesses include some moments where the writing occasionally tells rather than shows (particularly in exposition about Taylor's situation), and a few instances where the prose edges toward being slightly overwrought, though these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 80.0
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# My Apocalypse Buddy The thing about the end of the world, Midas reflected, was that nobody had warned him it would be so thoroughly *tedious*. The films had promised explosions, stirring speeches, and at minimum one dramatic moment where he could stand on a pile of rubble and shake his fist at the sky. Instead, he had spent the last six years mostly thinking about socks. "We're out of socks," he announced to the bunker at large, which consisted of three rooms, a ventilation shaft that whistled when it rained, and Chewy. Chewy sat on the milk crate that served as a command chair, the throne of the realm, and occasionally a footstool. His synthetic fur had gone the color of old tea. One ear drooped. The other had been chewed off years ago by something Midas preferred not to think about, which had only made the remaining ear more assertive by comparison. "I *know* socks aren't strictly essential," Midas said, responding to a comment Chewy had not made. "But my feet have feelings too, Chewy. My feet have been through a lot." Chewy's eyes flickered a soft, patient amber. This was Chewy's version of *hurry up and get to the point*. "Right. The Vendamart. We go in, we get socks, we check the back for those little cakes with the cream inside—" Amber pulsed to a warning orange. "—fine, we don't check for the cakes. We get socks. We come home. In and out. Fifteen minutes." The orange deepened to red. Red, in Chewy's extensive emotional vocabulary, meant either *I love you* or *you are about to die*, and Midas had long since given up trying to distinguish between them. --- The Vendamart squatted at the edge of what had once been a parking lot and was now a thriving ecosystem of weeds, rust, and something that might have been a raccoon if raccoons had decided, as a species, to give up on dignity. Midas approached with the cautious shuffle of a man who had learned, painfully, that the world was a series of holes waiting for his feet. Chewy rode in the front pocket of his pack, head poking out, scanning. The animo-tronics still worked after all these years, which was either a miracle of pre-collapse engineering or a small cruelty, depending on the day. "Clear?" Midas whispered. Green flicker. Clear. He slipped through the gap where the automatic doors had failed in their final act of automation and stood, blinking, in the gloom. The store smelled of damp cardboard and forgotten ambition. Somewhere, water dripped. "Socks are aisle seven," Midas murmured. "I remember the map. I am a man who remembers maps." He was not, in fact, a man who remembered maps. Socks turned out to be aisle twelve, which he discovered after visiting aisles two, eight, and a small section dedicated entirely to scented candles, a product whose purpose now seemed as remote and mysterious as cave paintings. He found the socks. He found, specifically, a bin of tube socks still sealed in their plastic, a preservation miracle that nearly brought him to tears. He was stuffing his seventh pair into his pack when Chewy's eyes went red. Not love-red. The other one. Midas froze. The sound came a moment later: a soft, wet clicking, like someone working their tongue against the roof of their mouth, only the tongue was bigger than it should be, and there were more of them than one mouth strictly required. "Chewy," Midas breathed. "Is that a—" Yellow-red-yellow-red. *Yes. Yes it is. Move.* He moved. He moved in the manner of a man whose legs have been informed of the situation slightly before the rest of him, which is to say he fell over a display of expired cough drops and slid approximately four feet on his stomach. The clicking got louder. Midas scrabbled up, clutched his pack, and ran, which in the aisles of the Vendamart meant mostly *colliding*. He collided with a shelf. He collided with a shopping cart that had been waiting six years for this exact opportunity. He collided, finally and spectacularly, with the back wall, where a door marked EMPLOYEES ONLY gave way under his shoulder and dumped him into the alley behind the store. The clicking did not follow. Clickers, in Midas's limited and preferably theoretical experience, did not like sunlight, doorways, or perhaps employees. He lay in the alley for a moment, breathing. "We got the socks," he told Chewy. Chewy's eyes glowed a steady, exhausted green. --- The journey home went sideways in the way journeys tend to, when one is bleeding from a shoulder and limping from an ankle one didn't remember twisting. It started raining. The rain turned the path into a slurry. The slurry hid a pothole. The pothole introduced itself to Midas's other ankle. By the time the bunker hatch came into view, Midas was being supported largely by spite and the cheerful red pulsing of Chewy's eyes, which had settled into what Midas had come to understand as the koala's version of a pep talk. He didn't know what the pulses meant, exactly. He suspected they were set, in the forgotten before-time, to indicate things like *tickle me* or *buy the extended warranty*. But out here, at the end of everything, they meant: *I am here. You are not alone. Keep going.* He fell through the hatch. He fell down the ladder. He fell, finally, onto the mattress, and lay staring at the ceiling while his various injuries filed their complaints in order of seniority. Chewy tumbled out of the pack and landed beside his head. One ear up. One ear gone. Eyes a soft, triumphant gold. "Seven pairs," Midas whispered. "Seven pairs of socks, Chewy." The koala's eyes flickered once, warmly, and went dark for the night. Outside, the world continued to end, slowly, as it had been doing for years, and would continue to do, tomorrow, after breakfast.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, philosophical observations about mundane aspects of extraordinary circumstances, and the gentle absurdism that characterized his work. The opening line about the apocalypse being "thoroughly tedious" immediately establishes the Pratchettesque tone, and the author maintains this voice consistently throughout. The story structure adheres well to the prompt requirements, beginning with deliberation about the mission, showing how it goes sideways, and returning the characters home worse for wear. The relationship between Midas and Chewy is cleverly developed through the koala's color-coded emotional responses, creating genuine character interaction despite Chewy being non-verbal. This device works particularly well and feels authentically Pratchett-like in its blend of technology and whimsy. The prose demonstrates strong technical skill with well-constructed sentences that flow naturally. Descriptive passages like "squatted at the edge of what had once been a parking lot and was now a thriving ecosystem of weeds, rust, and something that might have been a raccoon if raccoons had decided, as a species, to give up on dignity" showcase the author's ability to blend humor with vivid imagery. The story maintains narrative coherence throughout, with each mishap building logically from the previous one. The pacing is well-managed, moving from setup through action to resolution without feeling rushed or dragged out. The ending strikes an appropriate balance between the characters' immediate failure and small victory, avoiding unearned positivity while maintaining the gentle optimism typical of Pratchett's work. However, the story occasionally relies on telling rather than showing, particularly in establishing the post-apocalyptic setting. Some descriptions, while atmospheric, border on being slightly overwrought. The clicker encounter, while effective for creating tension, feels somewhat predictable as a post-apocalyptic trope. The emotional complexity could be deeper - while the relationship between Midas and Chewy is touching, it remains relatively surface-level. The dialogue, though limited, feels natural when present. The story succeeds in being emotionally engaging through its gentle humor and the genuine affection between the characters, though it doesn't reach profound emotional depths. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Bookshop on Wellfield Road The bell above the door gave a panicked jangle as he flung himself inside, slammed it shut, and pressed his back against it like a man fleeing a firing squad. "Close. Don't look at me. Pretend I'm not here." The woman behind the counter did not look up from the invoice she was annotating. "You've just told me you're here. Rather defeats the purpose." "Fair point." He risked a glance through the window, where two men with cameras the size of small artillery pieces were jogging past towards the castle. He exhaled. "Christ. Right. I'll just — browse. If that's alright." "That's generally what the shop is for." He pushed off the door and drifted into the nearest aisle, running a finger along the spines. He was waiting, she noted without looking, for her to recognise him. She could feel him waiting the way you feel someone breathing behind you in a queue. "Lovely shop," he offered. "Thank you." "Proper old-fashioned. Smells like — what is that? Vanilla and damp?" "Books and Cardiff." He laughed. It was, she admitted privately, a good laugh — the kind that had probably been insured at some point. She turned a page. "I'm Rhys, by the way," he said, appearing at the end of her counter with a paperback he clearly hadn't chosen on purpose. "Rhys Morgan." "Mm." "...Mm?" "Three-fifty for the Daphne du Maurier, if you want it." He blinked at the book in his hand as though it had materialised there. "Right. Yes. I — do you not —" He stopped himself, and she watched him decide, with visible effort, not to finish the sentence. "Do I not what?" she said, mildly. "Nothing. Nothing at all. I'm just a man buying a book." "Good. That's three-fifty." He fished out a card. She rang it through. He leaned on the counter with the easy slouch of a man who had leaned on a great many counters and found them accommodating. "And you are?" "Carys." "Carys." He said it like he was tasting it. "That suits you." "It's my name. It would be strange if it didn't." Something flickered across his face — the smallest crack — and then the grin reassembled. "You're not going to make this easy for me, are you?" "I wasn't aware I was meant to be making anything easy." "No. No, quite right." He took his card back. Lingered. "It's only that usually —" "Usually?" "Usually people —" He gestured vaguely, a gesture that took in himself, the shop, the whole unfair business of being recognisable. "Never mind." She put his book in a paper bag, folded the top down with two neat creases, and slid it across to him. And then, because she was not a monster, and because his ears had gone slightly pink, she said: "I know who you are, Mr. Morgan. I saw the film at Chapter last autumn. You were very good in it." He lit up like a chapel at Christmas. "You saw it? Did you — which bit did you —" "The one where you cried in the rain." "That was a good bit, that was —" "It was quite wet." He stared at her. Then he laughed, properly this time, startled out of himself, and she felt the laugh somewhere behind her sternum and very deliberately did not react to it. "You're awful," he said, delighted. "You're genuinely awful. Do you know how rare that is?" "I imagine not rare enough, for you." "Oh, vanishingly. People — people are so *nice*, Carys, you wouldn't believe. It's exhausting." He leaned both elbows on the counter now, the paper bag forgotten. "Everyone's so frightened of being the one who wasn't charmed. It makes them all charmed. It's dismal." "Poor you." "I mean it. It's —" "Poor, poor you. Hounded by affection. However do you cope." "Are you flirting with me or insulting me?" "Yes." He opened his mouth. Closed it. For the first time since the bell had rung, Rhys Morgan — who had, it was widely agreed, one of the three best jawlines in British television — appeared to have nothing to say. Carys turned back to her invoice. Her hand, she noticed with some irritation, was not entirely steady. She pressed the pen down harder. "What are you doing tonight?" he said. "Stocktaking." "Have dinner with me." "I'm stocktaking." "After stocktaking." "It takes all night." "Does it." "It does when I want it to." He was quiet a moment. She could feel him looking at her and she would not — absolutely would not — look up. There was a small, traitorous warmth climbing her neck. "Carys," he said, quite gently, without any of the varnish, "would you please look at me." She looked at him. He had nice eyes. She'd known this, of course — half of Wales knew this — but up close they were just eyes, tired round the edges, a little too hopeful. Not a film star's eyes. Just a man's. "I'm going to come back tomorrow," he said. "And the day after. And I'm going to keep buying books I don't need until you agree to have dinner with me. Just so you know. So you can plan your stock accordingly." "That's harassment, Mr. Morgan." "It's *patronage*, Miss —" "Lewis." "Miss Lewis." He picked up his bag. Backed towards the door. "Until tomorrow." "The photographers have gone." "I know." He opened the door. The bell jangled, more cheerful this time. "I just wanted you to know I wasn't only hiding." And then he was out on Wellfield Road, and Carys was alone in the shop with a slightly crooked invoice and a face that was, she discovered when she caught it in the window's reflection, smiling. "Oh, *bother*," she said, to no one, and turned the page.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with two well-defined characters whose personalities emerge clearly through dialogue and action. The characterization is particularly effective. Rhys Morgan is portrayed as a charming actor with a fragile ego who genuinely craves authentic connection, while Carys Lewis emerges as sharp-tongued but inwardly affected by his presence. The author skillfully shows Rhys's vulnerability beneath his practiced charm, especially in moments like when "his ears had gone slightly pink" and when he drops his polished persona to speak "quite gently, without any of the varnish." The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Carys's dry wit ("Books and Cardiff," "It's quite wet") contrasts nicely with Rhys's more effusive, performer-like speech patterns. The banter feels natural and serves the dual purpose of revealing character while building romantic tension. The Welsh setting is subtly but effectively established through references to Cardiff, Chapter cinema, and the castle, without becoming heavy-handed. The bookstore atmosphere is well-rendered through sensory details like the bell, the smell of "books and Cardiff," and the paper bag with "two neat creases." The romantic tension builds organically through their verbal sparring, with both characters' internal states revealed through small physical details and behavioral cues. The author successfully shows Carys's growing attraction despite her surface resistance, and Rhys's genuine interest beyond his usual charm offensive. However, the piece occasionally veers toward predictable romantic comedy territory, and some moments feel slightly constructed rather than entirely natural. The ending, while satisfying, follows expected beats for this type of scene. The prose is generally clean and effective, though it doesn't reach exceptional heights of literary distinction. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door swung inward on hinges that did not groan, and that alone should have told Aya everything. The underhold breathed out at them — a draught of old stone and lamp-oil and something sweeter beneath, like spoiled lilies. Gas jets hissed along the vaulted walls in their brass sconces, casting long, unsettled shadows that seemed to lean toward the trio as they stepped across the threshold. Aya's hand tightened on the haft of her blade. Katra, at her shoulder, had gone still in the way a cat goes still. Simeon, behind them both, was already muttering something foul under his breath. The chamber opened before them: a long gallery, pillars to either side, and at its far end the scythan reliquary squatted upon its plinth like a toad of black iron and older bone. It was unguarded. Which was to say — it was guarded by everything else. "Close the door behind you, dear. There's a draught." The voice came lazy and low from the shadow of the nearest pillar, and Aya's stomach fell clean through the flagstones. Mistress Vell stepped into the gaslight as though she had been poured there. She wore no chains. No irons. No bruises from the truncheons of the Watch. She wore, instead, a greatcoat of oxblood wool and a pistol at her hip — an ugly brass-framed thing, its cylinder already turned to live. Behind her, from behind every second pillar, men stepped forward in their oilcloth and leather, each with a shortened carbine or a cutlass held with the easy competence of those who had used such things more than once and recently. *Eight,* Aya counted, and her mouth went to ash. *Nine. Ten.* "Oh," Katra said, flatly. "Fuck." "Eloquent as ever, Katra-love." Vell's smile did not reach her eyes, which were the colour of wet slate and just as warm. "Drop them. The knives, the pistol, the little clever thing you keep up your sleeve, Simeon, yes, that one. On the floor, if you please. Slowly. I have a terrible nervous disposition this morning, and my lads are worse." They dropped them. There was nothing else to do. Steel rang on stone in a small, miserable chorus. "The raid—" Simeon began. "Theatre," said Vell. "Passable theatre. The Watch takes its coin like anyone else, and I had a notion to see who would come scuttling out of the wainscoting the moment I was supposed to be breathing my last in a cell at Gallowgate." Her gaze settled on Aya, and lingered. "And here you are. My little prodigy. My masked marvel. Step forward, pet." Aya did not move. "Step *forward.*" A carbine's hammer clicked back somewhere to the left. Aya stepped forward. *Think. Think, damn you. The immateria — no, not here, not without the chalk, not without time, they'd put three balls in me before I drew the first sigil —* "The mask, darling." "Mistress—" "The mask, Aya, or Boyle here takes your friend's knee off at the joint, and I do so hate the *screaming.*" Her hand — gloved, steady by some miracle her body was performing without her — rose to the edge of the lacquered half-mask. She felt the ribbon's knot give. She felt the cool air of the underhold touch her cheekbones, her brow, the small scar beneath her left eye that the portraitist had been paid handsomely to omit. There was a silence. Then Vell laughed — a single, astonished bark. "*Well.* Well, well, well. The Honourable Miss Aya Castellan-Reeve. In the flesh and in my cellar." She turned to her men as though sharing a marvellous joke. "Lads, we've been harbouring *nobility.* Hanged-witch's daughter, no less. They do say the apple doesn't fall, don't they." The word *witch* struck Aya harder than she had prepared for. She saw, for an instant that was not an instant, her mother's bare feet above the scaffold boards. She made her face still. "Mistress Vell." Her voice came out her mother's voice — the drawing-room voice, the voice for difficult vicars and inconvenient creditors. "Before you do anything precipitate, I would ask you to consider the arithmetic." "Oh, *arithmetic.* Listen to her." "My father is Lord Halvern Castellan-Reeve. He sits on the Admiralty board and three of the chartered companies. He has, at last audit, a personal fortune in excess of four hundred thousand sovereign. I am his only living child." She let the words fall one at a time, like coins on a counter. "A corpse is worth nothing. A daughter returned, with a discreet note and a discreet sum named, is worth a very great deal. Kill me and you have a body to dispose of and the Watch *genuinely* interested in your affairs for the first time in a decade. Ransom me and you are comfortable until the next war." Vell tilted her head. She was still smiling, but something behind the smile had sharpened to a point. "And your little friends?" "Part of the price. Non-negotiable." "*Aya—*" Simeon hissed. "Shut up, Sim." "She is *bargaining* us—" "*I said shut up.*" Vell's laugh this time was softer, and worse. She stepped close — close enough that Aya could smell the clove-oil in her hair and the gun-grease on her coat — and took Aya's chin between two gloved fingers, turning her face to the gaslight as one might turn a coin to check the stamp. "You clever, *clever* little thing," she murmured. "You've thought about this on the way down, haven't you. Whilst the rest of you was busy being terrified." "Constantly," Aya whispered. "Mm." Vell's thumb brushed her cheek, almost tender. "The trouble, pet, is that your name going about does rather ruin you. You understand that? Once I've written that discreet note, there is no more Honourable Miss Castellan-Reeve. There is only what's left after." "I know," said Aya, and was surprised to find it was true. "Bind them," Vell said, without looking away from her. "All three. And somebody fetch me a pen."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric worldbuilding within the whalepunk fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a tense confrontation scene that adheres closely to the prompt's requirements, incorporating all requested story beats while maintaining the specified Victorian gothic aesthetic. The prose quality is notably high, with evocative descriptions like "The chamber opened before them: a long gallery, pillars to either side, and at its far end the scythan reliquary squatted upon its plinth like a toad of black iron and older bone." The writing maintains consistent voice and tone throughout, effectively balancing Aya's internal thoughts with external dialogue and action. Character voices are well-differentiated and authentic to the setting. Mistress Vell emerges as a compelling antagonist with her calculated cruelty and theatrical flair, while Aya's aristocratic composure under pressure feels genuine. The dialogue crackles with tension and reveals character effectively, particularly in exchanges like Vell's mocking "Oh, arithmetic. Listen to her" and Aya's calculated response about her father's wealth. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from the ominous quiet entrance through the revelation of Vell's presence to the unmasking and negotiation. The author skillfully avoids the requested fight scene while maintaining dramatic momentum through psychological confrontation. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward purple ("poured there," "breathing my last"), though these don't significantly detract from the overall quality. The piece also relies somewhat on familiar tropes (the fake arrest, the aristocrat in disguise), though it executes them competently. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Aya's internal struggle between her aristocratic identity and guild loyalty, and her recognition that exposure means losing her former life entirely. The ending strikes an appropriately dark tone without feeling overwrought.
