+ Physical-spatial: Breaking Formation — Score: 85.5
Prompt:
**"Breaking Formation"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a street dancer from a rough neighborhood who's won a scholarship to an elite K-pop training academy in Seoul. Desperately out of place culturally and technically, they struggle with a militaristically precise choreography that contradicts everything about their improvisational background. The punishing rehearsal takes place under a famous instructor known for breaking students to remake them, who sees potential in the narrator's raw talent but not their attitude. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—each dance move, position shift, and spatial relationship must be tracked with precision throughout the studio. Paint an exceptionally vivid kinetic picture through descriptions that capture not just movement but the contrast between the narrator's fluid, expressive style and the mechanical precision demanded. Their internal monologue should reveal culture shock, class anxiety, and the threatening realization that success here might mean losing their authentic movement voice. Include technical terminology that highlights the collision of street and commercial styles, moments when muscle memory betrays them, and a crucial decision point about whether to conform or rebel. Physical clarity is essential—readers must be able to visualize exactly how bodies move through space, how rhythms align or clash, and how the protagonist's body struggles against unfamiliar discipline. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Breaking Formation The mirror runs the full thirty feet of the north wall, and I can see every single one of us — sixteen trainees in two rows of eight, spaced exactly 1.2 meters apart because Instructor Baek measured it with a laser pointer this morning. I'm in the back row, third from the left. My reflection looks wrong. Everyone else stands in "ready position": feet shoulder-width, knees microbent, hands curled loose at hip-bone height, chin tucked a precise fifteen degrees. My chin floats higher. My weight sits heavier on my left leg — an old battle-stance habit from cyphers back in Atlanta, where you stay ready to duck a cocky freeze or steal a slot. The speakers in each corner thump the count-in. Four towers, each one calibrated by some sound engineer so the bass hits simultaneously from every direction — but it's a hair too loud, pressing against the sternum, making the floor mirror tremble under our sneakers. "Five, six, seven, eight—" Sixteen bodies punch right arms out at shoulder level, palms flexed, elbows locked. The sound is almost audible — sleeves snapping in unison. My arm goes out too, but my elbow stays soft, my wrist lazy, my palm loose. Street habit. In a cypher, a locked elbow is a broken elbow. Here, a soft elbow is a disqualification. "Stop." Instructor Baek doesn't shout. He doesn't need to. The music cuts mid-phrase. He walks from his stool at the mirror's edge — forty feet away — straight down the aisle between the two rows, dress shoes clicking on marley. He stops at my row, pivots ninety degrees, and stands one meter from my right shoulder. "Elbow." I lock it. My whole arm goes rigid up to the deltoid, and the small muscles at the back of my shoulder — muscles I've never isolated in my life — cramp instantly. "Chin." I drop it. Fifteen degrees. I guess. "Weight." I shift to center. My left hip, which has carried me for nineteen years, complains. He walks back to his stool. Music from the four corners again. Count-in again. The choreography is eight-count blocks stacked into a sixty-four-count routine, and I've memorized the shapes but not the *quality*. On count one, we hit the arm. Count two, the arm retracts to a bent-elbow chamber at the ribs while the left foot steps forward eighteen inches — they literally showed us eighteen inches on a diagram. Count three, right foot slides to meet it, body dropping two inches into a demi-plié while both arms execute a sharp downward "point-press" — wrists broken, fingers taut. Count four, the body rises and the head snaps left, a clean ninety degrees. My head snaps — but my shoulders follow, just a breath late. In krump, in freestyle, the shoulders travel with the head because they're *telling* the head where to go. Here, the head moves alone. Decapitation aesthetic. Muscle memory drags my traps along and the whole line of me ripples when it should be laser-cut. Count five. The formation rotates: front row peels diagonally forward-left, back row slides diagonally back-right, creating a checkered diamond. I'm supposed to travel exactly three steps at a forty-five-degree angle to land on my mark — a tiny X of black tape I can feel under my left heel when I hit it clean. But I'm thinking about my head instead of my feet, and I overshoot by maybe six inches. My left shoulder brushes the right shoulder of the girl now beside me — Jiwoo, tiny, perfect, her body a compass needle. She doesn't flinch. Doesn't even breathe wrong. Her eyes in the mirror don't slide toward me. "Stop." The music dies. Baek walks again. This time he stops directly behind me, so close I can smell black coffee and some cedar cologne. "You." In English, for me. "You are dancing a solo inside my group." "I—" "Don't speak. Watch." He moves to my mark. His body — fifty years old, thin as wire — drops into the count-three plié with a precision that makes my chest hurt. Every angle is a decision. Every stillness is loaded. He runs counts one through eight and he is not expressing anything; he is *executing*. And somehow it's still beautiful. Terrifying and beautiful, like a machine that knows it's a machine and is proud. "Again. From top. You — watch first round. Join round two." I step off the floor. Stand against the mirror. Fifteen trainees reset. The music starts. And here's what happens to me, watching: I see my hood. I see the parking lot behind the Chevron where I learned to windmill on cardboard. I see my cousin Dre, who taught me that dance was the one thing nobody could take because it lived in the spine. I see every cypher I ever won by being *unpredictable*, by letting the music ride me instead of riding the music. And I see it all about to get sanded off, one locked elbow at a time, until I'm Jiwoo — flawless, identical, a compass needle pointing wherever the label wants. The eight-count comes around. I'm supposed to step back in. I don't. I stay against the mirror for one more count. Then I walk — not to my X, but to the front-center mark, the lead position, empty because we haven't been assigned it yet. Jiwoo's eyes finally find me in the mirror. Baek's head turns. I hit count one with a locked elbow. Perfect. Count two, chamber, step — exact eighteen inches, I can feel it. Count three, plié, point-press, wrists broken clean. And on count four, when the head snaps left, I let my shoulders follow. Just a breath. Just enough. A flavor. A signature. A hand-print left on the glass. The formation keeps moving around me. I see Baek in the mirror. He doesn't stop the music. He's watching. He hasn't decided yet. Neither have I.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional technical craft and strong adherence to the complex prompt requirements. The author successfully maintains precise physical coherence throughout, tracking every movement, position, and spatial relationship in the dance studio with mathematical precision. The use of specific measurements (1.2 meters apart, eighteen inches, forty-five-degree angles) and technical terminology creates a vivid kinetic picture that allows readers to visualize exactly how bodies move through space. The contrast between street dance fluidity and K-pop precision is expertly rendered through specific physical details - the protagonist's soft elbow versus locked joints, shoulders following head movement versus isolated head snaps, and weight distribution habits from cypher battles. The technical dance terminology effectively highlights the collision between styles without becoming inaccessible. The first-person voice is consistently authentic, capturing the protagonist's cultural displacement and class anxiety through internal observations that feel genuine rather than forced. The narrative successfully builds tension through the protagonist's physical struggles against unfamiliar discipline, culminating in a well-earned moment of rebellion that feels both inevitable and surprising. The prose is elegant and controlled, avoiding purple excess while maintaining rich descriptive power. Phrases like "decapitation aesthetic" and "compass needle pointing wherever the label wants" are precise and evocative. The pacing builds effectively toward the climactic decision point. However, the piece occasionally borders on being slightly overwrought in its precision - the hyper-detailed measurements, while serving the prompt's requirements, sometimes feel mechanical. The ending, while dramatically satisfying, leaves the protagonist's ultimate choice somewhat ambiguous, which may frustrate some readers seeking clearer resolution. The emotional complexity is strong, particularly in capturing the protagonist's internal conflict between conformity and authenticity. The muscle memory betrayals are convincingly portrayed, and the culture shock feels genuine rather than stereotypical. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Gothic-fantasy-humour: Court of the Dreaming — Score: 85.7
Prompt:
Court of the Dreaming: Territorial Dream Dispute. Write a dark fantasy scene with comedic elements set in the Courts of Dream where a case is being heard to determine ownership of a contested dreamscape. Mora, the Slavic nightmare spirit who sits on sleepers' chests causing paralysis and terror, has recently invaded and subverted a carefully crafted recurring dream of a schoolboy, Max. The dream originally featured the boy lying in a field of wildflowers with his crush, always culminating in a muddled almost-kiss and a pleasant confusion of emotions. This dream was the meticulous work of "First Love" (a rosy-cheeked, slightly bashful entity who crafts formative romantic experiences). However, Mora has recently infiltrated the dreamscape, transforming the scene so that when the girl playfully mounts the boy, she morphs into a terrifying yet somehow still confusingly attractive vision who proceeds to sit on his chest and feed on his breath. Mora's defense largely consists of the practical argument that "a girl's gotta eat too, you know?" Write this scene as it unfolds in the courtroom of the Dreaming, with Dream's demeanour, typically impenetrable, betraying a sense of irritation at having to adjudicate such matters. The comedy should derive from the farcical situation, familiar elements of courtroom drama intersecting with the fantastical world of the Dreaming, and the droll portrayal of the characters and their interactions. The humor should be dark and understated throughout. Dream is tempted to some mischief in the resolution of the case. Use descriptives sparingly when setting scene. Write for readability & engagement. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Court of the Dreaming: Territorial Dream Dispute The hourglass dripped. Not sand, exactly — something finer, like the pollen of an idea that had never quite bloomed. Each grain caught the light and lost it again before reaching the bottom. Dream of the Endless sat on a bench carved from a single petrified yawn. His expression suggested a headache, though he did not get headaches, and his robes suggested midnight, because they were. "Let us begin," he said, "and let us, if at all possible, be brief." First Love stood at the petitioner's podium, hands clasped, cheeks pink as a slapped apricot. He wore a soft cardigan. He had chosen it himself, that morning, in the mirror, and had worried about it. Opposite him stood Mora. Mora was not wearing a cardigan. Mora was wearing what can only be described as the last thing a dying man sees, tailored. Her hair moved in a wind nobody else could feel. She smiled at the bailiff — a small, squat nightmare with too many teeth — and the bailiff forgot, for three full seconds, how lungs worked. "Petitioner," said Dream. "State your grievance." First Love cleared his throat. "Your, ah. Your Lordship. The dream in question belongs to one Maximilian Kovač, aged thirteen years, two months. I have been cultivating it since spring. Wildflowers. A girl named Ana. A — a near-kiss. The confusion of — " He faltered, blushing harder. "— of the heart's first weather." "Quite," said Dream. "It is a formative piece," First Love went on, gathering courage. "I crafted the light myself. The pollen count is accurate to the region. I sourced the bees, Your Lordship. *Individually.*" "And the respondent's intrusion?" "She *sits on him,* Lord." He looked down at his notes, which were written in a round, careful hand. "She mounts the boy in place of Ana, and then she — she — " "I eat his breath," said Mora helpfully. "*She eats his breath,*" whispered First Love, aghast. Dream turned, slowly, to Mora. "Respondent. Your defence." Mora shrugged. It was a gesture that had, historically, started wars in three small kingdoms. "A girl's gotta eat, my Lord." There was a silence. The hourglass dripped. Somewhere in the back of the courtroom, a minor terror coughed politely into its claw. "That," said Dream, "is the entirety of your defence." "It's a good one," said Mora. "It's honest. You like honest, don't you? You've got that face." "I have," Dream said, "many faces. None of them are inclined toward flattery." "Noted," said Mora. "My Lord." She curtsied. The curtsey was somehow obscene. First Love, emboldened by his opponent's informality, pressed on. "Your Lordship, with respect — the dream was *mine.* I built it. I placed every — every petal. She came in uninvited, she — she *wears Ana's face,* Lord, and then she *becomes herself,* and the boy wakes up gasping, and he — he — " His voice dropped. "He still kind of *likes it,* Lord. That's the worst part. He wakes up and he feels things he doesn't have words for, and now when he thinks of Ana in the waking world he also thinks of — of *her* — and it's all tangled." "Tangled," Dream repeated. "Ruined," said First Love. "*Enriched,*" said Mora. "Your Lordship, she is *corrupting foundational affective architecture —* " "Your Lordship, he is *coddling the child.*" Mora stepped forward. Her shadow did not follow her; it stayed where she'd left it, watching. "The boy is thirteen. Thirteen, Lord. In my country he would already be sleeping with one eye open and knowing why. Love without fear is a cake without flour. It does not rise. It sits on the plate, pink and stupid, and no one remembers it in the morning." "I *want* them to remember it in the morning," said First Love, wounded. "They remember *mine,*" said Mora. "Because you *traumatise* them — " "Because I am *interesting.*" "*Enough,*" said Dream. The word was not loud. It did not need to be. The hourglass stopped dripping for a courteous half-second, then resumed. Dream steepled his fingers. He looked at First Love — earnest, trembling, cardiganed. He looked at Mora — smiling with too many rows of intention. He was tempted. He was, in point of fact, very tempted. The case was beneath him. These small quarrels of function, these demarcation disputes between the minor staff of sleep — Lucien handled them, usually, and Lucien was not here, because Lucien had asked for one afternoon, *one afternoon, my Lord, to reshelve the Ds,* and Dream had said yes, and now here he was, adjudicating a squabble over a thirteen-year-old boy's chest. He could, he thought, rule for First Love. Restore the field. Send Mora off with a warning and a fine measured in stolen sighs. He could rule for Mora. Let the boy grow up with a complicated relationship to pretty girls and the weight of small bodies, and produce, eventually, poetry. Or. "I find," said Dream slowly, "for neither party." Both petitioners looked up. "The dream shall be *shared.*" First Love made a small, wounded sound. "On alternating nights," Dream continued, "the dream shall unfold as the petitioner intends. Wildflowers. The near-kiss. The pleasant confusion." He paused. "On the other nights, the respondent may feed. But she shall feed *only* after the almost-kiss. Not before. The boy is to have his tenderness first. His terror, second." "My Lord — " began First Love. "And," said Dream, "on the seventh night, neither of you shall attend. The boy shall dream of something else entirely. A bicycle. A dog. A door he cannot open. I leave the selection to chance." Mora grinned. "That's cruel, Lord." "Yes," said Dream. "I am tired, and I am cruel, and I wish to return to my library. Case concluded." He rose. The hourglass dripped on, indifferent, and in a small bedroom somewhere in a small town, Max turned over in his sleep and did not yet know what was coming for him, in all its forms.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully creating a dark fantasy courtroom scene with comedic elements set in Neil Gaiman's Sandman universe. The author captures Dream's characteristically aloof and irritated demeanor effectively, particularly through lines like "I am tired, and I am cruel, and I wish to return to my library." The characterizations are well-executed and nuanced. First Love is portrayed as earnest and slightly bumbling ("He had chosen it himself, that morning, in the mirror, and had worried about it"), while Mora embodies dangerous sensuality with memorable descriptions like wearing "the last thing a dying man sees, tailored." Dream's weariness and subtle cruelty come through authentically. The prose style is elegant and restrained, following the instruction to use descriptives sparingly while still creating vivid imagery. Phrases like "a bench carved from a single petrified yawn" and "the hourglass dripped. Not sand, exactly — something finer, like the pollen of an idea that had never quite bloomed" demonstrate sophisticated writing without becoming overwrought. The dialogue feels natural and character-appropriate, from Mora's casual "A girl's gotta eat, my Lord" to First Love's flustered academic language about "foundational affective architecture." The exchanges build tension while maintaining the darkly comic tone. The resolution is particularly strong, showing Dream's mischievous nature through a Solomon-like judgment that satisfies neither party while creating more complexity for the dreaming boy. This feels earned and consistent with the character's established personality. The piece maintains excellent pacing and coherence throughout, building from the initial setup through the arguments to the resolution without meandering. The dark humor emerges naturally from the absurd situation rather than feeling forced. Minor weaknesses include some moments where the prose occasionally edges toward being slightly precious, though it generally stays on the right side of that line. The ending, while thematically appropriate, could have used slightly more development of the implications. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Fanfic-crossover: Blood Moon — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Blood Moon Alucard & Yennefer Write a story where Alucard (Hellsing) encounters Yennefer (The Witcher) in medieval Europe. Yennefer's magical abilities intrigue the vampire, while she's both repulsed and fascinated by his monstrous nature. Their power dynamic shifts constantly as they circle each other, neither willing to show weakness first. **Alucard (Hellsing)** Alucard is an ancient, immensely powerful vampire who works for the Hellsing Organization. He can regenerate from almost any wound and shape-shift at will. He's sadistic, arrogant, and takes pleasure in toying with his enemies before killing them. Despite being a monster, he despises weak vampires who can't accept their nature. He wears a distinctive red coat, wide-brimmed hat, and orange sunglasses. He uses massive handguns loaded with silver bullets. He seeks worthy opponents who can challenge him and potentially end his immortal existence. Famous quote: "The bird of Hermes is my name, eating my wings to make me tame." **Yennefer of Vengerberg (The Witcher)** Yennefer is a powerful sorceress who magically altered her appearance to hide her congenital hunchback. She's ambitious, stubborn, and manipulative when necessary. She has violet eyes and smells of lilac and gooseberries. Despite her cold exterior, she desperately wants to reverse her infertility caused by magical transformation. She's centuries old but appears young. She's politically savvy and refuses to be underestimated. She has a sarcastic sense of humor and little patience for fools. Though she rarely shows it, she's capable of deep love and loyalty. Famous quote: "I'm not sweet and I'm not a maiden in distress in need of saving." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Blood Moon A broken cart wheel lies abandoned near the village gates, hinting at a hurried departure. The villagers fled before sundown—wise of them. Yennefer steps over the splintered spokes, her violet eyes narrowing at the silence. No dogs. No livestock. Only the wind dragging through empty doorways and the metallic tang of old blood on the air. She smells him before she sees him. Not lilac and gooseberries—*him*. Copper and cordite, grave-dirt and something colder beneath it all. Something that should not be walking. "You're bold, sorceress, coming here alone." The voice unspools from the shadow of the well. He steps into the moonlight as if the moonlight were merely a courtesy he extends to her—a tall figure in an impossible red coat, wide-brimmed hat tilted low, circular glasses burning orange despite the dark. His grin is too wide. His teeth are wrong. Yennefer does not flinch. She has perfected the art of not flinching over the course of several lifetimes. "And you're presumptuous, assuming I'm alone because you cannot sense anyone else." She tilts her head. "Or perhaps you assume I need company to handle a creature like you." "*Creature.*" He savors the word, rolling it across his tongue. "How diplomatic." "Would you prefer *abomination*?" "I'd prefer honesty." "Then: abomination." She lets a small smile curve her lips, sharp as a paring knife. "Though the coat is very fine. A concession to vanity is almost human of you." Alucard laughs. It is not a pleasant sound. It rolls through the empty square and sets the shutters rattling on their hinges, and somewhere in the black pines beyond the village a murder of crows takes flight. He steps closer. She does not step back. Chaos gathers at her fingertips, invisible to mortal eyes but blazing to his—a lattice of violet fire woven tight against her skin. "You're shielded," he observes, delighted. "Layered, even. Clever girl." "Woman." "Older than you look?" "Older than you'd guess." "I'd guess *very* old." His grin widens further than a human jaw should permit. "I can smell the centuries on you. Under the perfume." Yennefer's nostrils flare—the only betrayal she allows herself. *Perfume.* As if he could see through the glamours to the hunched girl beneath, to the surgeries of bone and blood that had made her what she is. She tastes iron at the back of her throat and does not know if it is fear or fury. She suspects, as usual, it is both. "And I," she says softly, "can smell what you've done. The village. How many?" "None tonight." He sounds almost disappointed. "They ran. I was merely... passing through. The blood you smell is older. A lord who made the mistake of thinking his title meant something to me." "How democratic of you." "I contain multitudes." She laughs before she can stop herself—a single sharp exhalation—and immediately regrets it. His head cocks like a hawk's. He has heard the crack in her armor and he is, she realizes with cold certainty, the sort of thing that lives for cracks. "There." He takes another step. Now she can see her own face reflected twice in the orange lenses, small and pale and unimpressed. "That was real. Do it again." "No." "*Please.*" "Vampires don't say please." "This one does. When he means it." His gloved hand rises, slow, deliberate, and she lets him—*lets* him, she tells herself—bring it close enough to her cheek that she can feel the cold radiating from his palm. He does not touch her. He knows better. The chaos around her would burn his hand to ash and they both know it and that shared knowledge is its own kind of intimacy, uglier than most. "You're curious about me," he says. "I'm a scholar. I'm curious about many unpleasant things." "And I'm curious about *you.*" His voice drops. "You *rebuilt* yourself. I can see the seams. Bone-magic, blood-magic—you carved the girl you were into the woman you wanted and you left something behind in the doing." The words land like a slap. Her shield flares white-hot for an instant—he smiles, pleased to have drawn it out—and then she reins it in, because she refuses, *refuses*, to give him that. "Everyone leaves something behind," she says coolly. "You left your humanity in a shallow grave somewhere. I left a deformity in a surgeon's basin. Mine was the better trade." "Was it?" "Shall I list the men I've ruined since? It's a long list." "Oh, I believe you." His teeth flash. "I can see them on you too. Ghosts cling to sorceresses like burrs." "And what clings to *you*, Alucard?" She says the name as a wager. She had overheard it spoken in fear by a priest three villages back, and she throws it now to see if it hits. It does. He goes very still—the particular stillness of a predator deciding whether to be amused or offended—and then he removes his hat and sweeps her a bow so deep and so mocking it could curdle milk. "*The bird of Hermes is my name,*" he murmurs, "*eating my wings to make me tame.* You've done your homework, witch." "Sorceress." "My apologies." They circle each other now, slow, deliberate, the broken cart wheel between them like the remnant of some failed peace treaty. Neither will turn their back. Neither will look away. Yennefer feels her pulse in her teeth and knows he can hear it, and she knows that he knows she knows, and somewhere in that infinite regress of knowing is a door she is not yet foolish enough to open. "Travel with me a while," he says suddenly. "I'm bored. You're not." "I'd sooner travel with a plague." "Plague is dull company. I am not." She considers him. The blood moon is rising, fat and red above the pines, and neither of them casts the shadow they ought to. "One night," she says at last. "And if you bore me, I burn you." "*Promise?*" Yennefer smiles, and this time she lets it reach her eyes. "Don't I always?"
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical writing ability and a sophisticated understanding of both characters. The author successfully captures the essence of Alucard and Yennefer, maintaining their distinctive voices and power dynamics throughout the encounter. The characterization is particularly strong. Alucard's sadistic playfulness and predatory nature come through clearly in his dialogue and actions, while Yennefer's defensive pride and magical prowess are well-established. The author effectively shows rather than tells their personalities through their verbal sparring and physical positioning. The power dynamic shifts naturally as requested, with neither character gaining clear dominance. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative imagery like "moonlight were merely a courtesy he extends to her" and "chaos gathers at her fingertips." The writing maintains an appropriately dark, gothic atmosphere that suits both characters and the medieval European setting. The dialogue crackles with tension and wit, feeling authentic to both characters. However, the piece occasionally veers into purple prose territory with phrases like "unspools from the shadow" and overly elaborate descriptions. Some metaphors feel forced, and there are moments where the writing becomes slightly overwrought in its attempt to maintain the gothic atmosphere. The story structure is solid, building tension through their verbal and psychological duel before reaching a natural conclusion with Yennefer's conditional agreement to travel together. The ending feels earned rather than forced, growing organically from their mutual fascination and wariness. The author successfully adheres to most instructions, maintaining third person present tense throughout and hitting the approximate word count. The medieval European setting is established through environmental details like the abandoned village and cart wheel. Minor weaknesses include some repetitive phrasing and occasional moments where the elaborate prose style slightly impedes narrative flow. The piece also relies heavily on dialogue and internal observation, which works well for these characters but limits action sequences. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 14 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Fanfic-crossover: Prison Strings — Score: 83.0
Prompt:
Prison Strings Jolyne Cujoh & Makima Write a story where Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure) and Makima (Chainsaw Man) cross paths at a supernatural prison facility. Jolyne is immediately suspicious of Makima's falsely sweet demeanor, while Makima is intrigued by Jolyne's Stand ability. Their battle of wits escalates dangerously as Jolyne realizes how manipulative Makima truly is. **Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure)** Jolyne is the daughter of Jotaro Kujo and the first female JoJo protagonist. Her Stand, Stone Free, allows her to unravel her body into string that she can control. She's hot-headed, foul-mouthed, and doesn't take disrespect from anyone. She was framed for murder and sent to prison. She has a distinctive butterfly tattoo on her arm and wears her hair in two buns. Despite her tough exterior, she's deeply loyal to her friends and family. She can be impulsive but is highly adaptable in battle. She inherited her father's ability to stay cool under pressure but is more emotionally expressive. Famous quote: "If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney!" **Makima (Chainsaw Man)** Makima appears as a young woman with auburn hair and ringed yellow eyes. She's the Control Devil who can dominate anyone she considers beneath her (nearly everyone). She maintains a pleasant, soft-spoken demeanor that masks her manipulative, cold nature. She can create contracts with humans and devils, often exploiting their desires. She's obsessed with Chainsaw Man and wants to control him. She keeps numerous people and devils as pets on leashes. She's practically immortal due to a contract with the Prime Minister. She views humans as lesser beings but mimics human emotions perfectly. Famous quote: "It's okay. Dogs are supposed to die for their owners." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Prison Strings The Green Dolphin Street Prison has a new wing. No one talks about how it got built, or why certain inmates get transferred there without paperwork. Jolyne Cujoh knows better than to ask. She walks the yard with her hands in her pockets, two buns bobbing as she moves, the butterfly tattoo on her arm catching the late afternoon sun. Chain-link fences rattle whenever an inmate passes by, a metallic shiver that crawls up Jolyne's spine. Something is wrong here. Has been wrong since that new transfer arrived yesterday. "Excuse me." The voice is soft, polite, almost musical. Jolyne turns. A woman stands by the fence, her auburn hair braided neatly, her prison jumpsuit somehow looking pressed. She smiles. Her eyes are yellow and ringed like a target. "You're Jolyne Cujoh, aren't you? I've heard such interesting things about you." "Yeah? And who the hell are you?" "Makima." The smile doesn't waver. "I was hoping we could talk. You seem like someone who could help me understand this place." Jolyne's jaw tightens. Every instinct her father ever drilled into her is screaming. *Stay cool under pressure.* But cool doesn't mean stupid. This woman's voice is too smooth, like cream poured over glass. "I don't do charity. Find another sucker." "Oh, I'm not asking for charity." Makima tilts her head, and for a moment Jolyne swears the yellow rings in her eyes *move*, like gears clicking into place. "I'd like to offer an exchange. I hear your Stand is quite remarkable. Stone Free, isn't it? You unravel your own body into string." The hair on Jolyne's neck stands up. "Who the fuck told you that?" "I notice things." Makima's smile widens by a single calibrated millimeter. "For instance, I notice you're very loyal. You miss your father. You have friends here you'd die for. That's a beautiful quality, Jolyne. It makes you... useful." "*Useful?*" Jolyne takes a step forward. Around the yard, she realizes the other inmates have gone quiet. Too quiet. A woman she recognizes—tough, mouthy, someone who'd never shut up—is staring at Makima with blank adoration. Two guards by the tower stand oddly still. A pigeon on the fence hasn't moved in a full minute. *What the hell.* "Relax," Makima says gently. "I only want to help. You were framed, weren't you? I could help prove it. All I'd need is a small favor. A promise. Something minor." "If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney," Jolyne snaps. "I don't know what kinda game you're running, lady, but I've met enough smiling snakes to smell one a mile off. My dad taught me better." Makima laughs. It is the most human sound Jolyne has ever heard, which is exactly why it's wrong. "Your father. Jotaro Kujo. Yes, I know about him too." She raises her finger, almost lazy. "Kneel, please." Jolyne's knees twitch. For a single horrifying instant, something vast and invisible presses down on her spine, trying to bend her like a straw. A weight heavier than any Stand she's ever felt—not punching, but *commanding*, as if the world itself has decided she is a smaller thing than this woman. Her left leg buckles. "*Stone Free!*" String erupts from her arm. Her skin unravels in neon-green thread, shooting across the yard, wrapping the fence, anchoring her upright. Jolyne grits her teeth and forces her leg straight. Sweat pours down her face. Makima blinks. Just once. But Jolyne sees it—the flicker of genuine surprise behind those yellow rings. "Oh my," Makima murmurs. "How interesting. You resisted." "You—you tried to—" Jolyne's breathing is ragged. "What the fuck *are* you?" "I'm someone who likes dogs, Jolyne." Makima's smile returns, but something beneath it has shifted, gone cold and clinical. "Dogs that behave. Dogs that come when called. I thought you might make a lovely addition. Your Stand is so versatile—strings are such useful things. Leashes, essentially." Jolyne's strings tighten around the fence, around Makima's ankle, around a nearby bench post. A web. A trap. She wraps a loop invisibly around her own ears, muffling—just slightly—the sound of Makima's voice. If words are the weapon, she'll dull the blade. "Lady, I've been chained up by my own government, beaten up by Stand users twice my size, and dumped in a prison full of psychos. You think a creepy smile and a voice trick is gonna put a collar on me?" "Not a trick. A privilege." Makima's voice lilts. "Everyone eventually understands. It's simply a matter of finding what they fear losing." Jolyne's strings twitch. She thinks of her father. Of Ermes. Of Foo Fighters. Her chest squeezes, and the strings wobble in answer. *She's reading me. Every damn second I look at her, she's reading me.* Jolyne looks away. Forces her gaze to the dirt, to the rattling chain-link, to anywhere but those yellow rings. Her father's voice rises in her memory: *Don't let your enemy dictate the rhythm. Change the beat.* "Yeah? Well here's what I fear losing." She grins, wild and feral. "My patience." The strings snap taut. The bench rips from its concrete mooring and swings across the yard in a vicious arc. Makima sidesteps it with unnatural, floating grace—but Jolyne's real attack is already in motion: a second strand coiled around the guard tower's alarm switch, yanking hard. The yard erupts. Sirens scream. Guards who were standing mannequin-still jolt awake, confused. The inmates' trance breaks into panicked motion. The spell, whatever it was, fractures under the noise. Makima's smile finally, finally falters. "Clever girl." "Yeah. Runs in the family." Jolyne backs away, strings retracting into her arm, the butterfly tattoo pulsing. "We're not done, lady. But next time, I'm bringing friends. And I'm wearing earplugs." Makima watches her go, head tilted, yellow rings slowly rotating. "Oh, Jolyne," she says softly, to no one. "I do so enjoy a dog that bites." The chain-link fence rattles as Jolyne passes, and somewhere in the sound is the faintest echo of string being pulled tight—a warning, a promise, a noose waiting for the right neck.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This story demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding. The author successfully captures both characters' distinctive voices and mannerisms - Jolyne's hot-headed defiance and profanity-laden speech contrasts effectively with Makima's deceptively polite manipulation. The supernatural prison setting feels authentic and atmospheric, with subtle details like the unexplained new wing and the eerie quiet of controlled inmates building tension effectively. The characterization is particularly strong. Jolyne's immediate suspicion and resistance feels true to her nature, while her tactical thinking (using strings to muffle Makima's voice, triggering the alarm to break the spell) shows both her adaptability and intelligence. Makima's gradual revelation of her true nature - from false sweetness to cold calculation - is well-paced and menacing. The author understands both characters' power sets and uses them creatively. The prose maintains good pacing and atmosphere throughout. Descriptions like "cream poured over glass" for Makima's voice and the imagery of yellow rings "like gears clicking into place" are evocative without being overwrought. The action sequences are clear and dynamic, particularly Jolyne's escape using the alarm system. However, the story has some minor weaknesses. The supernatural prison concept, while atmospheric, isn't fully explored or explained. Some dialogue moments feel slightly forced, particularly Jolyne's famous quote insertion which feels somewhat shoehorned in. The ending, while satisfying, doesn't quite reach the "dangerously escalating battle of wits" promised in the prompt - it's more of a first encounter that establishes the dynamic. The story succeeds in creating genuine tension and showcasing both characters' abilities and personalities. The power dynamic between them feels authentic - Makima's overwhelming control ability versus Jolyne's resourcefulness and resistance. The writing maintains consistent quality throughout without major technical flaws. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 16 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 16.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# SCP-████ Supplementary Document: Personal Log of Dr. E. Vance **Classification:** Temporal Anomaly (Suspected) / Cognitohazard (Suspected) **Status:** Under Review, Internal Investigation Division --- I found the first note on a Tuesday, tucked beneath my coffee mug in Lab 7. My handwriting, unmistakably — the same cramped lowercase *e*, the loop I can never quite close on my *g*. It read: > *Don't let them open Locker 14 on the 9th. Whatever Kowalski says, the override protocol is wrong. You have time. Please remember this.* I assumed a prank. We had no Locker 14 in our wing. Dr. Kowalski worked two sublevels below me and we'd exchanged perhaps twelve words in three years. I photographed the note, logged it per protocol, and submitted it to Analytics expecting nothing. Analytics returned the report forty-eight hours later. The ink contained trace elements of cesium-137 and an unidentified organic compound not present in any pen, marker, or writing instrument manufactured or stocked by the Foundation. The paper was ordinary — torn from one of my own legal pads, the fibers consistent with the batch in my desk drawer. That was when they assigned Dr. Ramirez to shadow me. --- The second note appeared inside my jacket pocket on the 4th. I had not taken the jacket off that day except to hang it on my chair, which was in my sightline for seven hours. > *You're going to think I'm you. I am. Or I was. The 9th — Kowalski is wrong about the coolant. It isn't coolant. It breathes. Write this down somewhere they can't find it.* I showed it to Ramirez immediately. She was kind about it, which was worse than if she had been clinical. She asked whether I had been sleeping, whether I recalled writing anything in a fugue state, whether I had any personal history of dissociation. I said no to all three, truthfully, and she nodded, and then she requisitioned a cognitive resonance scan. The scan showed nothing. No memetic contamination, no foreign cognitive signatures, no unusual neurological activity. I was, by every metric the Foundation possessed, exactly myself. --- The notes came faster after that. Three on the 5th. Five on the 6th. By the 7th I was finding them folded into pages of reports I had not yet filed, slipped between books I had not yet pulled from the shelf. The handwriting was deteriorating. The later ones slanted hard to the right, pressed so deep the paper tore in places. > *they told me it was contained they told me it was contained it wasn't contained it was listening the whole time* > *Kowalski isn't real. Check her badge photo. Check it against 2019. They changed her. Or we did.* > *I can't remember mother's name. Can you? Try. Try right now.* I tried. I could not. I sat at my desk for a long time after that one. Eventually I pulled up my personnel file, which listed my emergency contact as Margaret Vance, and I looked at the name and felt nothing — no recognition, no rejection, only the flat blankness of a word I had read but never spoken. I did not know if this meant the note was correct, or if reading the note had carved the hole it claimed to describe. Ramirez, when I told her, said gently that false memory implantation was a known signature of several contained entities, including SCP-████ and SCP-████-J, the latter of which had been decommissioned but whose effects were occasionally reported as residual. She suggested I take leave. I refused. If this was temporal — if some version of me was genuinely trying to warn me — then leaving was the worst possible action. If it was psychological, then leaving changed nothing about my perception, only my access. She wrote that down. I watched her write it down. --- **Note recovered from inside sealed evidence bag, 8th, 03:47:** > *you keep thinking there are two options. temporal or cognitohazard. there is a third. what if it's both. what if the thing in locker 14 learned how to write in your hand because it watched you long enough. what if I'm not you. what if I never was. but the ink — check the ink — the ink is from after* The evidence bag had been sealed in my presence, logged, placed in a secure drawer, and not reopened. The note was inside it. The seal was intact. Analytics confirmed the same trace elements. They also noted, in a line buried in the appendix, that the cesium-137 isotope ratio suggested the ink had been manufactured approximately sixty to ninety years *from now*. I read that line four times. --- The 9th is tomorrow. I do not know if there is a Locker 14. I have searched every locker room in three sublevels and found none numbered higher than nine. Dr. Kowalski's badge photo matches her 2019 record perfectly; I checked. She does not recognize me when we pass in the hall, which is unremarkable, because we have never spoken. Ramirez believes I am being manipulated. She has drafted a recommendation for my reassignment and, if I resist, my sedation. I have read the draft. She left it on her desk where I could see it. I do not know if that was accidental or a kindness. I found one more note an hour ago, in my own handwriting, in ink that will not exist for half a century: > *whatever you decide tomorrow, I decided it too. it didn't work. I'm sorry. I'm so sorry. try the other thing.* I do not know what the other thing is. I am going to sleep now, if I can. I am going to trust that the man who wakes up tomorrow is the same one writing this. I have no way to verify that. I have no way to verify anything. The pen I am writing with is blue. Ordinary. I watched myself buy it. I think I watched myself buy it. --- **[END LOG — Dr. Vance failed to report for duty on the 9th. Investigation ongoing.]**
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while effectively maintaining the requested first-person researcher perspective. The author successfully captures the paranoid, uncertain atmosphere characteristic of good SCP fiction, with the handwritten notes serving as an effective narrative device that becomes increasingly unsettling. The piece excels in its gradual escalation of psychological tension. The progression from mundane discovery to existential horror feels natural and earned, with each note revelation building logically on the previous ones. The detail about the researcher forgetting their mother's name is particularly effective, creating genuine uncertainty about whether this represents temporal manipulation or psychological breakdown. The writing maintains consistent voice throughout, balancing clinical Foundation terminology with the researcher's growing desperation. The prose is clean and purposeful, avoiding unnecessary flourishes while still creating vivid imagery through specific details like the "cramped lowercase e" and "loop I can never quite close on my g." However, the piece has some structural weaknesses. The middle section becomes somewhat repetitive in its pattern of note discovery and analysis. While this repetition serves the psychological deterioration theme, it occasionally feels mechanical rather than organic. The character of Dr. Ramirez, while serving her function, remains somewhat flat and could have been developed with more nuance. The ending is appropriately ambiguous and unsettling, avoiding the trap of over-explanation while still providing narrative satisfaction. The final uncertainty about the pen purchase effectively reinforces the theme of unreliable perception. The technical details about cesium-137 and isotope ratios add scientific credibility without becoming overly technical. The piece successfully creates the requested temporal paradox elements while maintaining plausible deniability about whether events are supernatural or psychological. The integration of Foundation protocols and procedures feels authentic to the SCP universe. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 14.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 85.5
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# SCP-XXXX: "The Ordered House" **Object Class:** Euclid (pending reclassification — see Addendum XXXX-E) **Special Containment Procedures:** All known instances of SCP-XXXX-1 (the doors) are to be sealed with Foundation-standard Type-7 locks. MTF Epsilon-6 ("Village Idiots") is tasked with identifying new instances, which manifest at an average rate of 2.3 per month in abandoned structures worldwide. Civilian witnesses are to be administered Class-B amnestics. Exploration of SCP-XXXX-2 (the archive) is suspended pending Overseer review, following events described in Exploration Log 14. **Description:** SCP-XXXX-1 designates a series of unmarked wooden doors appearing in abandoned or condemned buildings. Doors measure 210 cm × 84 cm and bear no hardware save a recessed iron ring. Notably, 31% of recovered instances exhibit weathering, grain warping, and oxidation patterns inconsistent with their host structures — a door in a 1974 Soviet housing block in Pripyat was dated by dendrochronology to approximately 2,400 years of age. The door predates the building by over two millennia. SCP-XXXX-2 is the space accessed through SCP-XXXX-1. It is an archive of indeterminate size — lidar scans have failed to locate terminal walls in any direction — consisting of shelving, filing cabinets, and reading desks in configurations that violate Euclidean geometry. Corridors loop into themselves. Staircases descend to ceilings. Researchers report the persistent sensation of being "filed." The archive's contents reference a prior global civilization, referred to in its own documents as the **Concord**, whose collapse is attributed to an event designated only as "the Unseating." No explicit description of this event has been recovered, though contextual references suggest a catastrophic containment failure on a scale exceeding any known K-Class scenario. --- ### Exploration Log 7 — Team Perspective Team Delta (four personnel, led by Senior Researcher Okafor) entered SCP-XXXX-1 instance at a collapsed schoolhouse in Misiones, Argentina, at 04:17 local time. The team emerged onto a gallery overlooking rows of shelving receding beyond visual range. Okafor noted the air smelled of dry paper and ozone. Agent Reyes described the ceiling as "breathing, sort of — like it was deciding how tall to be." At approximately forty minutes in, the team located a reading desk bearing documents left open, as though recently consulted. Researcher Vance photographed the following excerpts before retrieval: > *"...and in the ninety-fourth year of the Concord, the Bureau of Quiet Things was reconstituted under civilian oversight, its charter broadened to include phenomena heretofore classified as devotional. The founding principle — Secure, Contain, Preserve — remained unaltered, as it had remained unaltered through the four previous reconstitutions..."* > > — Fragment, *Administrative Histories*, shelf-code ᛟ-114-b The team recognized the founding principle immediately. Agent Reyes reportedly laughed, then stopped laughing. A second document, bound in what Vance described as "something that wasn't quite leather," contained personnel files. One bore the name, photograph, and biometric fingerprint of Dr. Okafor himself, listed as *Archivist-Third-Grade, Department of Unsettled Matters*. The file was dated to a calendrical system the team could not parse, but a marginal annotation in modern English read simply: **"Prior term: 1412–1447 CE. Performance satisfactory."** Okafor ordered immediate withdrawal. The file was not recovered; Vance's photographs of it developed blank. --- ### Exploration Log 11 — Team Perspective Team Kappa entered an instance in a derelict cannery in Hokkaido. Within the archive, at a distance estimated at six kilometers from ingress, the team observed figures in hazmat suits of unfamiliar design — seams sealed with wax, visors of smoked glass, respirators trailing cables into the shelving itself. The figures were removing documents. Not reading them, not cataloguing — *removing*. Files were deposited into lead-lined carts and wheeled into corridors that closed behind them. When Team Kappa approached, the figures froze. One raised a gloved hand in what Agent Perera interpreted as a gesture of warning. Then, in unison, they abandoned their carts and fled. Team Kappa examined the remaining documents. Most concerned the Unseating. All were partially redacted — not by ink, but by excision; entire paragraphs had been physically cut from the pages and the edges cauterized. What remained: > *"...the failure occurred not in the object but in the observers. It is our enduring error to believe that containment is a wall. Containment is an agreement..."* > > *"...and when the agreement was broken, we did not notice for some years, for the world continued in its ordinary habits, as a corpse may remain warm..."* The carts themselves vanished between photographs. --- ### Exploration Log 14 — Team Perspective (Partial) Team Sigma entered an instance in Detroit. At approximately ninety minutes, they located a private reading alcove sealed with a cord of braided copper. The cord had been cut. Inside, on a desk, lay a sign-in ledger. The most recent entries, in chronological order: - **O5-4.** "Routine review." Dated three months prior. - **O5-9.** "Consultation re: successor protocols." Dated five weeks prior. - **O5-1.** No stated purpose. Dated eleven days prior. - **O5-7.** "I am sorry." Dated nine days prior. None of these visits appear in Foundation access logs. No travel records corroborate them. When questioned, four of the named Councilors declined to respond; one (O5-7) has not been located since the ledger's recovery. Team Sigma's withdrawal was expedited after Senior Researcher Okafor, reviewing the ledger by radio, ordered the archive be considered hostile. --- ### Addendum XXXX-E: Note from Site Director Harlow We are not the first. We may not be the current. The doors are older than their buildings because the archive is older than the idea of buildings. It has been sorting us for longer than we have been sorting anything. The hazmat figures are not stealing the files. I believe they are *containing* them — from us. I believe they have done this before, for four previous iterations of this organization, and that the act of their fleeing when approached is itself a kind of protocol. A courtesy. The Overseers have been reading their own obituaries. I have requested the doors be left alone. My request has been denied.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while delivering genuinely unsettling cosmic horror. The author successfully captures the bureaucratic tone that makes SCP entries effective, using technical language and procedural formatting to ground the supernatural elements in institutional reality. The concept is genuinely creative - doors older than their buildings leading to an archive that has been cataloguing multiple iterations of the Foundation across millennia. The exploration logs effectively build tension through accumulating revelations, from the discovery of personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago to the unauthorized O5 Council visits. The hazmat entities removing documents adds an active threat element that elevates the mystery. The prose maintains clinical precision while incorporating evocative details like "the persistent sensation of being 'filed'" and "something that wasn't quite leather." The author avoids purple prose while still creating atmospheric moments, such as the ceiling "breathing, sort of - like it was deciding how tall to be." Character actions feel believable within the SCP universe - researchers following protocols, immediate withdrawal upon discovering anomalous personal files, and the escalating concern of leadership. The dialogue is minimal but effective, particularly Agent Reyes laughing then stopping when confronted with the implications. The structure builds effectively through the exploration logs, each revealing more disturbing information. The final addendum from Site Director Harlow provides a chilling conclusion that recontextualizes everything - the hazmat figures aren't stealing files but containing them from the Foundation, suggesting the current organization may be the threat. Some minor weaknesses include occasional exposition that could be more subtle, and a few moments where the implications could be developed further. The piece occasionally tells rather than shows, particularly in explaining the archive's impossible geometry. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise compelling piece that successfully balances cosmic horror with institutional bureaucracy. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17

+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 87.0
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Weight of Cloth The cloak had been a mistake. Iyen understood this within twenty paces of the embassy gate, but by then turning back would have meant admitting to the guards that they had been right to raise their eyebrows, and so Iyen walked on into the city of Hesh-Aruun with the heavy grey wool hanging from their shoulders like a confession. The locals did not wear cloaks. The locals, in the blue afternoon, wore almost nothing: a wrapped skirt of bleached cloth, a sash across one shoulder for the women of the taller caste, a kind of mesh vest for the broader, shorter people who moved between the market stalls with the unhurried competence of those who have always done the carrying. Iyen had studied the holographs. The holographs had not prepared them for skin. So much skin, in so many shades of ochre and copper and the pale, almost lavender tone of the high-lineage Aruunei, whose bones had lengthened across twelve generations until they were no longer, strictly speaking, the same creature as the stevedores at the harbor. The briefings had described two subspecies and a managed tension between them. In the market of Hesh-Aruun, Iyen counted at least five distinct morphologies in the first street, and none of the tensions seemed managed. A tall lavender woman was laughing with a short coppery man over a basin of live eels. A child with the broad chest of the mountain people was being scolded, fondly, by a slender elder whose fingers had one extra joint. The child bore the elder's face. Lineage, then, was not what the Ministry reports had claimed it was. Lineage mixed. Lineage laughed. Iyen bought a skewer of something oily and salt-burned from a vendor who named a price, saw the cloak, and named a different price. Iyen paid it without haggling. The vendor looked disappointed. "Offworlder," said a voice at Iyen's elbow. It was not hostile. An old man, sitting on an overturned crate, was peeling a fruit with a blade worn thin by decades. "You walk without a guard." "I walk with my own feet," Iyen said, which was the polite form in Aruunei, and the old man smiled because they had gotten it right, and also because they had gotten it slightly wrong, the way a child does. "Your coat is very heavy," the old man said. "Yes." "Our sun is not your sun." "No." The old man nodded as though a treaty had been concluded between them, and went back to his fruit. Iyen walked on. They saw, in the hour that followed, three things the briefings had denied. A shrine where members of every morphology laid the same small white stones. A school where the children were not sorted by lineage but by the month of their naming. A wall, recently whitewashed, beneath which the faint outline of a slogan still showed through: *One blood, many shapes.* Someone had tried to erase it. Someone else, Iyen suspected, had whitewashed it gently, so the words would not quite disappear. By the time Iyen returned to the embassy, the cloak was soaked through at the collar and Command was waiting. Command was a voice in a room. The Coordinator had not left the inner chambers in three cycles; she spoke through the wall-panel in the tone of a woman who had once been disappointed by a lover and had resolved, thereafter, to be disappointed only by subordinates. "Envoy Iyen. You left the compound unescorted." "Yes, Coordinator." "You walked in the lower market in a cloak that identifies you at two hundred paces as an offworld diplomat." "Yes." "You will explain." Iyen considered several answers and chose the one that was true. "The briefings were wrong. I wanted to see what was wrong about them before I accepted an audience in which I would be expected to repeat them." There was a silence. It was not a forgiving silence, but it was not the other kind either. "You will go," said the Coordinator at last, "to the Minister of Science. Tonight. He has requested you. I had intended to refuse him until you were better prepared. You have removed that option." "Who is he?" "A man who has never held a pipette in his life. He was Minister of Revenue before, and Minister of the Interior before that. He selects his portfolios the way a sculptor selects chisels. Science is his current chisel. Do you understand what I am saying?" "He wants to cut something." "He wants to cut *you*, Envoy. To see what you are made of, and whether it can be used. Do not flatter him. Do not contradict him. Do not—" a pause, a dry sound that might have been humor "—wear the cloak." --- The Minister's receiving room smelled of resin and cold stone. He was lavender-pale, tall as a mast, and when he rose to greet Iyen he did so with the considered slowness of a man who has learned that his height is a weapon and prefers to sheathe it. "Envoy. Sit. You took a walk today." "I did." "Tell me what you saw." Iyen sat. They had rehearsed three answers on the way over and discarded all of them in the corridor. They said, "I saw a city that does not match its reports." The Minister's eyes did something small and pleased. "And which, Envoy, do you propose to correct? The city, or the reports?" It was the question, Iyen understood, on which everything turned: whether they would speak as an envoy of an empire that preferred its maps to its territories, or as a person who had, that afternoon, paid too much for a skewer and been forgiven for it by an old man with a thin knife. "The reports," Iyen said. "If you will help me." The Minister smiled, and the smile was not kind, but it was interested, and interest, Iyen thought, was the beginning of everything.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Ursula K. Le Guin's literary style, successfully capturing her anthropological approach to science fiction, her spare yet evocative prose, and her focus on cultural observation over action. The author has created a compelling narrative that follows the prompt's requirements while maintaining literary quality throughout. The characterization of Iyen is particularly well-executed. The protagonist emerges as curious, thoughtful, and willing to challenge assumptions - traits that drive the narrative naturally. The detail of the cloak serves as both a practical plot device and a symbol of cultural disconnect, showing rather than telling us about Iyen's outsider status. The character's decision-making feels authentic, from the initial choice to explore alone to the final commitment to truth over diplomatic convenience. The world-building is exceptional, presenting a complex society that subverts the official reports through concrete, observed details. The author avoids the trap of over-explaining, instead letting readers piece together the social dynamics through Iyen's encounters. The various human subspecies are presented with nuance - not as warring factions but as integrated communities with natural diversity. The prose style successfully emulates Le Guin's voice without feeling derivative. Sentences like "Lineage mixed. Lineage laughed" capture her rhythmic, almost poetic approach to language. The writing maintains restraint, avoiding purple prose while still creating vivid imagery. The description of the Minister as moving "with the considered slowness of a man who has learned that his height is a weapon" exemplifies the author's skill at character revelation through physical detail. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building world details. The conversation with the old man establishes cultural protocols while showing Iyen's earnest attempt to connect. The exchanges with the Coordinator and Minister demonstrate different power dynamics effectively. The political tension builds organically, culminating in the Minister's test question about correcting cities versus reports. This moment feels earned rather than forced, growing naturally from Iyen's earlier observations and choices. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - neither overly optimistic nor pessimistic, but suggesting possibility through mutual interest. Minor weaknesses include some moments where the anthropological observations feel slightly heavy-handed, and the Coordinator's characterization relies somewhat on familiar tropes. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall quality. Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
